title: Heatwave hits tourism in Bagalkot - The Hans India
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Karnataka
full_text:
Known for its rich Chalukyan heritage, ancient temples and spiritual destinations, Bagalkot district is witnessing a sharp decline in tourist footfall due to the intense summer heat. With temperatures soaring close to 40 degrees Celsius, tourists are staying away from popular destinations such as Badami, Aihole, Pattadakal and Kudalasangama, affecting both tourism and local businesses.
The famous Badami cave temples, the architectural marvels of Aihole and Pattadakal, and the spiritual centre of Kudalasangama associated with Basavanna are usually crowded during the holiday season. However, the scorching heat has left many of these tourist spots nearly deserted. Residents and tourists alike are avoiding outdoor travel as the district continues to experience extreme temperatures.
According to tourism department data, the number of foreign tourists visiting some heritage destinations has declined compared to last year. Between January and April 2025, Aihole recorded 1,971 foreign visitors, while the number dropped to 1,687 during the same period in 2026. Pattadakal too saw a decline in tourist arrivals this year.
Badami, however, witnessed a slight increase in foreign tourist visits. The site recorded 2,849 foreign tourists between January and April 2025, compared to 3,091 visitors during the same period this year. Despite this marginal rise, the overall tourism activity across the district has slowed considerably.
The decline in visitors has also severely impacted local businesses dependent on tourism. Hotels, lodges, restaurants, tender coconut vendors and cold drink shops are reporting poor business compared to previous years. Traders say afternoon business has almost disappeared as people avoid stepping out in the extreme heat.
Tourism Department in-charge Deputy Director Gopal Hittalamani said that basic facilities, including drinking water arrangements, have been provided at major tourist destinations. “Tourist Mitras are also available for emergency assistance. However, due to the harsh summer conditions, the number of visitors has reduced significantly,” he said. Overall, the severe heatwave has affected Bagalkot’s tourism sector and the livelihood of many small traders who depend on tourist activity during the holiday season.
